summary: Mitchell Hashimoto demonstrates how to use Zig's comptime features to create type-safe subsets of tagged unions, solving a real problem in Ghostty where keybind actions need to be handled separately by terminal and application code. The naive solutions—using catch-all else clauses or explicitly listing ignored cases—either sacrifice compiler safety or result in verbose, poorly self-documenting code. By leveraging comptime type generation with @typeInfo and @Type builtins, he creates a ScopedAction function that generates union types containing only actions belonging to a specific scope. The solution provides compile-time exhaustiveness checking, self-documenting function signatures, and zero runtime cost for the type generation itself.
key_points: Tagged unions with catch-all else clauses lose compiler safety when new cases are added, but exhaustively listing ignored cases is verbose and doesn't express intent in the type signature
Zig's comptime allows generating new types at compile time by introspecting existing types with @typeInfo and reifying new ones with @Type
The scope() function that classifies actions doesn't need to know whether it runs at comptime or runtime—comptime executes the full language
The inline else pattern in switch statements generates code for each unhandled case while providing the tag as a compile-time known value
This approach provides compiler-enforced exhaustiveness, self-documenting function signatures, and centralizes the scope classification in one maintainable location
money_quotes: The problem is that the Action union contains both terminal-specific actions and application-wide actions.
When there is a catch-all case, the compiler can't error if I add a new action to the Action union and forget to handle it.
Since Zig has comptime code execution, and comptime code execution is the full language, we can use functions that take parameters at comptime and return things like... types.
Note that our scope() function above has no idea if it is being executed in comptime or runtime. It doesn't care. It's just a normal function. Comptime is fucking cool that way.
All of this logic happens at compile time. There is no runtime cost to doing any of this.

I'm a type pragmatist. I believe leaning on the type system to enforce correctness is a powerful tool. At the same time, I believe some languages take this too far and make the type system a burden. Zig strikes a nice balance for me.
key_insight: Zig's comptime enables generating specialized subset types from tagged unions at compile time, providing both type safety and self-documenting APIs without runtime overhead.
